title: LangChain Chat App with FastAPI and Vite React
tagline: Build a tiny full‑stack AI chat app from scratch using LangChain, FastAPI, and a Vite‑powered React UI.
description: In this tutorial we will create a small but complete full‑stack application. The backend is a FastAPI server that uses LangChain with a fake LLM to echo user messages. The frontend is a modern React app built with Vite that talks to the API. The whole project lives in a single monorepo with a clean layout, type‑hints, and tests.
problem_statement: You want to experiment with LangChain and see how it can be wired into a web service, but you don’t have a full‑stack template to start from. Building the plumbing yourself each time is tedious.
end_state:
At the end of the tutorial you will have a runnable monorepo containing:
- A FastAPI backend exposing `/health` and `/chat` endpoints.
- A LangChain chain that processes the user message.
- A React UI that lets a user type a message and see the assistant’s reply.
- Automated tests for the backend.
- Clear instructions for running both servers together.
prerequisites: Basic Python knowledge (functions, modules, type hints).
Node.js (v20+) and npm installed on your machine.
learning_outcomes: Set up a modern Python project with `pyproject.toml` and type hints.
Create a FastAPI service and add CORS middleware.
Integrate LangChain using a fake LLM for quick iteration.
Bootstrap a Vite + React frontend and proxy API calls.
Run both backend and frontend concurrently in development.

stack.name: Python
stack.purpose: Core language for the backend and LangChain.

stack.name: FastAPI
stack.purpose: Lightweight async web framework for the API.

stack.name: Vite
stack.purpose: Fast dev server and bundler for React.

best_practices: Pin all dependency versions in `pyproject.toml` to avoid surprise breakages.
Keep the backend pure Python and the frontend pure JavaScript – no mixing of concerns.
Add CORS middleware early to prevent browser‑side request errors.
Write at least one test per endpoint before adding new features.
Use Vite’s proxy feature instead of hard‑coding backend URLs.
intro_transcript: Hey everyone! Welcome back to ProjectPalAI. Today we’re going to build a tiny full‑stack AI chat app from the ground up. We’ll spin up a FastAPI backend that uses LangChain with a fake LLM – no API keys required – and then we’ll create a sleek React UI with Vite that talks to our API. By the end of this 3‑hour tutorial you’ll have a complete monorepo you can run locally, a couple of automated tests, and a solid foundation for any LangChain‑powered web service. Let’s dive in!

steps.title: Create the repository skeleton
steps.goal: Initialize a new git repo, add a `pyproject.toml` and a README.
bullets: Run `git init` and create a clean folder structure.
Add `pyproject.toml` with pinned dependencies.
Write a short README that explains the project.
steps.speaker_notes: First things first – open your terminal and create a new folder called `langchain_fastapi_fullstack`. Inside that folder run `git init` so we have version control. Then we’ll add a `pyproject.toml` file – this is the modern way to declare Python dependencies. I’ll copy a minimal configuration that pins FastAPI, Uvicorn, LangChain, Pydantic, and Httpx. After that we create a `README.md` with a one‑sentence description and a table of contents. This step gives us a clean starting point and makes sure anyone cloning the repo knows what they’re looking at.

steps.title: Add the backend package
steps.goal: Create a Python package for the FastAPI service.
bullets: Create `src/backend/__init__.py` (empty).
Create `src/backend/main.py` with a basic FastAPI app and a health endpoint.
steps.speaker_notes: Now we’ll set up the backend package. Inside `src` we create a folder called `backend` and add an empty `__init__.py` so Python treats it as a package. In `main.py` we import FastAPI, instantiate the app, and expose a simple `/health` route that returns `{ "status": "ok" }`. This gives us something we can run immediately with `uvicorn src.backend.main:app --reload`. Running it now should show a JSON response at `http://127.0.0.1:8000/health`. If you see `{"status":"ok"}` you’re good to go.

steps.title: Add CORS middleware
steps.goal: Allow the React dev server to call the FastAPI API without cross‑origin errors.
bullets: Import `CORSMiddleware` from `fastapi.middleware.cors`.
Add the middleware with `allow_origins=['*']` for development.
steps.speaker_notes: When we later spin up the React app on a different port, the browser will block requests to our API unless we enable CORS. FastAPI makes this trivial – we import `CORSMiddleware` and add it right after creating the `FastAPI` instance. For now we’ll allow all origins, methods, and headers – perfect for local development. Remember to keep this permissive setting only in dev; in production you’d lock it down to your actual domain.

steps.title: Integrate LangChain with a fake LLM
steps.goal: Create a LangChain chain that echoes the user’s message and expose it via a `/chat` endpoint.
bullets: Add `src/backend/model.py` that builds a `LLMChain` using `FakeListLLM`.
Expose a POST `/chat` route that receives a `message` and returns the chain’s reply.
steps.speaker_notes: Time to bring LangChain into the picture. In `model.py` we’ll build a tiny chain that uses `FakeListLLM` – this is a built‑in LangChain class that pretends to be an LLM without any external API keys. The chain simply echoes the input back to us. We then import a helper `get_response` function from `model.py` into `main.py` and add a POST `/chat` endpoint. The endpoint receives a JSON payload with a `message` field, runs it through the chain, and returns a JSON response containing the `reply`. Because we’re using a fake LLM, the whole thing runs instantly and works offline, which is perfect for a beginner tutorial.

steps.title: Write backend tests
steps.goal: Add a couple of pytest tests that verify the health and chat endpoints.
bullets: Create `tests/test_backend.py`.
Use `TestClient` from FastAPI to call the endpoints.
Assert that `/health` returns status ok and `/chat` returns a string reply.
steps.speaker_notes: Testing is a habit you’ll thank yourself for later. We’ll create a `tests` folder and add `test_backend.py`. Using FastAPI’s `TestClient` we can make HTTP calls against our app without starting a server. The first test hits `/health` and checks the JSON payload. The second test posts a message to `/chat` and verifies that we get a `reply` field back. Run the tests with `pytest -q` – you should see two passing tests. If anything fails, double‑check the import paths and the request payloads.

steps.title: Bootstrap the Vite + React frontend
steps.goal: Create the frontend folder, install dependencies, and add the Vite config.
bullets: Run `npm create vite@latest src/frontend -- --template react`.
Replace the generated `index.html` with a minimal version that loads `/src/main.jsx`.
Add a `vite.config.js` that proxies `/api` to the FastAPI server.
steps.speaker_notes: Switching over to the UI side – we’ll use Vite because it gives us instant hot‑module reloading and a tiny configuration. Inside the repo run `npm create vite@latest src/frontend -- --template react`. This scaffolds a React project under `src/frontend`. We’ll simplify the generated `index.html` to just a root div and a script tag that points at `src/main.jsx`. The most important part is the Vite config: we tell Vite to proxy any request that starts with `/api` to `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. This way our React code can call `/api/chat` and Vite will forward it to the FastAPI backend, keeping the same origin from the browser’s perspective.

steps.title: Build the React UI
steps.goal: Create `main.jsx`, `App.jsx`, and an API helper that talks to the backend.
bullets: `main.jsx` renders the `App` component.
`App.jsx` holds state for the conversation, a form to send messages, and a display area.
`api.js` wraps `fetch('/api/chat')` and returns the `reply` field.
steps.speaker_notes: Now we write the actual UI. In `src/main.jsx` we bootstrap React with `ReactDOM.createRoot`. The `App` component is where the magic happens – we keep an array of message objects, each with a `role` (user or assistant) and `content`. The form at the bottom lets the user type a message; on submit we optimistically add the user message to the list, clear the input, then call our `sendMessage` helper. `sendMessage` lives in `api.js` and uses the Fetch API to POST to `/api/chat`. When the response arrives we push the assistant’s reply onto the message list. Simple, clean, and fully typed with React’s modern hooks.

steps.title: Run the full stack together
steps.goal: Start the FastAPI server and the Vite dev server, then test the chat UI.
bullets: In one terminal run `uvicorn src.backend.main:app --reload`.
In another terminal `cd src/frontend && npm install && npm run dev`.
Open `http://localhost:5173` in the browser, type a message, and see the echoed reply.
steps.speaker_notes: Everything is wired up – let’s see it in action. Open two terminal windows. In the first one start the backend with `uvicorn src.backend.main:app --reload`. You should see the usual Uvicorn startup logs and the `/health` endpoint responding. In the second terminal `cd src/frontend && npm install && npm run dev`. Vite will spin up on port 5173 and automatically open a browser tab. Type something like “Hello world” into the input box and hit Send. The UI will show your message on the right and, a moment later, the assistant’s reply on the left – which in our fake LLM case is just an echo of what you typed. If you see that, congratulations – you have a working full‑stack app!

steps.title: Add a loading indicator (optional challenge)
steps.goal: Show a spinner while the request to the backend is pending.
bullets: Add a `loading` boolean state in `App.jsx`.
Set `loading` to true before calling `sendMessage` and false after the response.
Render a simple “Thinking…” message or CSS spinner when `loading` is true.
steps.speaker_notes: User experience matters – let’s give the user feedback while the backend processes the request. We’ll add a `loading` state hook in `App.jsx`. When the form submits we set `loading` to true, then after the `await sendMessage` resolves we set it back to false. In the render block we conditionally show a small “Thinking…” paragraph or a CSS spinner when `loading` is true. This small tweak makes the app feel more responsive and demonstrates a common pattern in React apps.

steps.title: Wrap‑up and next steps
steps.goal: Summarize what we built and point to extensions.
bullets: Review the project layout and the code we wrote.
Suggest swapping the fake LLM for a real OpenAI or HuggingFace model.
Mention Dockerizing the backend and deploying to Vercel or Render.
steps.speaker_notes: That’s a wrap! We just built a complete, runnable full‑stack AI chat app in under three hours. We saw how to structure a monorepo, set up a FastAPI service, integrate LangChain with a fake LLM, write a couple of unit tests, and spin up a modern React UI with Vite. From here you can replace the `FakeListLLM` with a real model – for example `OpenAI` or a HuggingFace transformer – and you’ll have a production‑ready assistant. You could also containerize the backend with Docker, add authentication, or deploy the frontend to Vercel and the API to Render. The sky’s the limit. Happy coding!

outro_transcript: Thanks for watching! If you enjoyed this tutorial, give it a thumbs up, subscribe for more project‑based coding videos, and drop a comment if you run into any issues. The full source code is linked in the description, and you’ll find a `README.md` with all the commands you need to get started. Keep experimenting, keep building, and I’ll see you in the next video.
how_to_run:
```bash
# 1️⃣ Install Python dependencies (optional, pyproject already pins them)
python -m pip install .

# 2️⃣ Run the FastAPI backend
uvicorn src.backend.main:app --reload
# The API will be available at http://127.0.0.1:8000

# 3️⃣ Open a new terminal for the frontend
cd src/frontend
npm install            # install React, Vite, etc.
npm run dev            # starts Vite on http://localhost:5173

# 4️⃣ Open your browser to http://localhost:5173, type a message, and watch the reply appear.
```

challenges.title: Add input validation to the chat endpoint
challenges.description: Prevent empty messages from reaching the LangChain chain and return a 400 error with a helpful message.
challenges.build_process: Modify `ChatRequest` to add a Pydantic validator that strips whitespace and raises a `ValueError` if the resulting string is empty. Update the `/chat` endpoint to catch the validation error and return a `HTTPException` with status code 400.
challenges.solution_walkthrough: We added a Pydantic validator on the `message` field. The validator strips whitespace and raises a `ValueError` if the resulting string is empty. FastAPI automatically converts that `ValueError` into a 422 response, but we catch it explicitly in the endpoint to return a clearer 400 status with our custom message. This prevents the LangChain chain from being called with an empty string and gives the client immediate feedback.

challenges.title: Add a unit test for the model function
challenges.description: Ensure that `get_response` returns a string containing the original user input.
challenges.build_process: Create a new test file `tests/test_model.py`. Import `get_response` from `src.backend.model` and assert that the returned reply includes the input text.
challenges.solution_code:
# --- tests/test_model.py ---
from src.backend.model import get_response

def test_get_response_contains_input():
    user_input = "Testing model"
    reply = get_response(user_input)
    # The fake LLM echoes the input inside the response string.
    assert user_input in reply
challenges.solution_walkthrough: The test imports the `get_response` helper directly and feeds it a known string. Because our fake LLM returns a templated response that includes `{input}`, the reply should contain the original `user_input`. The assertion guarantees that the LangChain chain is wired correctly. Running `pytest -q` should now show three passing tests.
